reason: The paper explicitly states it used "an actual impurity reduction importance measure" from a ranger Random Forest, citing Nembrini et al. 2018 (a paper on bias corrected Gini/impurity importance) - this is genuinely an impurity family measure, not permutation importance or SHAP. The resulting ranking (trip purpose=100%, gender=21%, region=10%, state/employment/age=5% each) is the direct empirical basis for the paper's headline policy conclusion that "purpose specific buffer sizes need to be employed," and for the secondary claim that gender is the "second most important determinant," so magnitude/ranking is unambiguously central, not incidental. The only other statistical analysis present is univariate Wilcoxon/Kruskal Wallis tests (Table 3), but these show ALL six explanatory variables are statistically significant (p less than 0.001 or p=0.007) - they establish that each variable's marginal association is nonzero, not the relative magnitude/ranking among variables, so they cannot corroborate why trip purpose ranks about 5x above gender or why gender ranks about 2x above region. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held out validation of the selected feature ranking appears anywhere in the text. One mitigating nuance worth flagging: citing Nembrini et al. 2018 suggests they may have used ranger's bias corrected "impurity_corrected" variant rather than naive Gini/MDI, which would reduce (though not eliminate) cardinality driven inflation of the 3 to 4 level trip purpose variable relative to the binary predictors - but this detail is not confirmed in the visible text (only in an unavailable supplement), and even a corrected impurity measure is not among the adequate corroboration methods specified, nor does it address interaction charging or nonlinear effect conflation. On the visible evidence, none of the required corroboration types are present, so the flag stands.
